sample.title: Kensuke's Kingdom
sample.plot: Young Michael, shipwrecked on a remote island, must adapt to life alone. Over time, he feels another presence, learning that this world is home to both unimaginable danger and beauty.

categories.label: far-flung islands reached by sea
categories.description: Across the Pacific, distance governs life: many places lie days from the nearest port, and boats remain the surest link to supplies, news, and rescue. What you'll see: open water without end, small craft and cargo vessels, isolated beaches, and the feeling of being cut off from the wider world.

categories.label: war memories lingering in the Pacific
categories.description: The Second World War is recent enough that its wreckage, losses, and personal scars still shape how people understand these islands. What you'll see: abandoned traces of conflict, uneasy recollections of Japanese expansion and defeat, and landscapes that seem peaceful while carrying the weight of recent violence.

categories.label: colonial outposts giving way slowly
categories.description: Many Pacific islands still live within imperial or former imperial arrangements, with outside powers setting borders, trade links, and official authority from afar. What you'll see: remote settlements tied to foreign ports, signs of rule arriving from elsewhere, and local life unfolding at a distance from metropolitan centres.

categories.label: island life shaped by scarce provisions
categories.description: On small islands, everyday survival depends on what can be gathered, fished, grown, or carefully preserved, because replacement goods are hard to come by. What you'll see: makeshift shelters, rationed stores, foraging along the shore, fresh water as a precious need, and tools used again and again.

categories.label: weather and sea as daily masters
categories.description: In the Pacific, wind, currents, storms, and heat are not background but ruling conditions that decide travel, labour, and safety. What you'll see: sudden squalls, punishing sun, rough surf, drifting debris, and people reading clouds, tides, and the behaviour of the sea.

categories.label: thick island wilderness beyond settlements
categories.description: Even where people have passed through, many islands remain dominated by dense vegetation, cliffs, reefs, and animal life rather than roads or built order. What you'll see: tangled forest paths, hidden coves, steep shorelines, bright birds and sea creatures, and terrain that feels both beautiful and perilous.

categories.label: Japanese lives scattered after defeat
categories.description: In the years after 1945, some Japanese people remain marked by displacement, separation, and the collapse of the empire that once stretched across the Pacific. What you'll see: solitary habits, improvised living in remote places, silence around the past, and a guarded relation to the wider world.

categories.label: human presence dwarfed by ocean nature
categories.description: Pacific island settings often place people inside a vast natural world where reefs, tides, animals, and seasons are stronger than any settled claim. What you'll see: empty horizons, beaches littered by what the sea delivers, close attention to creatures and plants, and a constant sense of human smallness.
